page.uc-repeat.title: Persistent Cart for Repeat-Customer Shopify Stores
page.uc-repeat.desc: Repeat customers are logged in by definition — exactly who cross-device sync serves. Persistent Cart keeps their carts alive across return visits and devices.
page.uc-repeat.h1: Repeat buyers are already logged in — so sync their carts
page.uc-repeat.lede: Repeat customers are the ideal fit for cross-device cart sync, because they're logged in by definition — and sync works for signed-in shoppers. When a returning buyer's cart survives across visits, devices, and browsers, you remove a small, recurring friction that quietly chips away at retention and lifetime value.
page.uc-repeat.s1.h: Why are repeat customers the ideal fit for cart sync?
page.uc-repeat.s1.b:
Because cross-device cart sync only works for signed-in shoppers — and repeat customers are signed in by definition. The single requirement that excludes one-time guests is already met by the buyers who come back: they have accounts, they log in, and they're exactly the people the feature is designed to serve.

That alignment is the whole point. For a store built on repeat business — subscriptions, replenishment, loyal regulars — most of your valuable traffic is logged in. Persistent Cart keys each cart to the customer ID and your store domain, so a returning buyer's cart is saved on every change and restored whenever and wherever they sign back in. The audience and the mechanism fit perfectly.
page.uc-repeat.s2.h: Do carts survive between a customer's return visits?
page.uc-repeat.s2.b:
Yes. For a signed-in customer, Persistent Cart saves the cart and restores it on their next visit — even days later, even on a different device or browser. A regular who adds a few items on their phone on Monday and comes back on a laptop on Thursday finds the cart still there, rather than an empty one.

This is different from Shopify's default, where the cart lives in a browser cookie lasting about two weeks and tied to a single browser. That cookie doesn't follow the customer between devices, and it isn't anchored to their account. By tying the cart to the customer instead, Persistent Cart makes picking up where you left off work the way returning buyers already expect from large retailers like Amazon.
page.uc-repeat.s3.h: How does a persistent cart help retention and lifetime value?
page.uc-repeat.s3.b:
By removing a small friction that repeats on every visit. Each time a returning customer has to rebuild a cart they'd already started, that's a moment of annoyance and a chance to give up. Multiply that across a base of regulars and many visits, and a cart that simply persists protects a measurable slice of repeat revenue.

The expectation is already set: 88% of shoppers want a consistent experience across channels and devices (Bazaarvoice, November 2024). Meeting it for your logged-in regulars makes the store feel reliable and considered, which is what keeps people coming back. Retention compounds — so a friction you remove once pays off across every future visit that customer makes.
page.uc-repeat.s4.h: Is it a good fit for subscription and replenishment stores?
page.uc-repeat.s4.b:
Yes — repeat-purchase models are among the best fits. Subscription, replenishment, and loyalty-driven stores run on logged-in customers making frequent return visits, which is precisely the audience cross-device sync serves. The more of your revenue comes from people who come back and sign in, the more a persistent cart is working with your traffic rather than at the edges of it.

Setup is one click and one switch: install from the Shopify App Store, then enable the app embed in your theme editor — no theme-code editing. It then runs quietly, with a silent additive merge on login by default, so returning customers just find their cart waiting. Every feature is included on every plan, priced to your Shopify subscription.
page.uc-repeat.faq.q1.q: Does Persistent Cart only help logged-in customers?
page.uc-repeat.faq.q1.a: Yes — cross-device and cross-visit sync works for signed-in customers, not anonymous guests, because the cart is keyed to the customer ID. For repeat-customer stores that's a feature, not a limit: your returning buyers are logged in by definition, so they're exactly who the app serves. Guests still get Shopify's standard single-browser session; your regulars get their cart everywhere they sign in.
page.uc-repeat.faq.q2.q: How does it affect customer lifetime value?
page.uc-repeat.faq.q2.a: It removes a recurring friction — rebuilding a lost cart — that repeats across a loyal customer's many visits. There's no published rate to put on that, but the logic is direct: fewer abandoned restarts means fewer dropped repeat orders, and retention compounds over time. With 88% of shoppers wanting a consistent cross-channel experience (Bazaarvoice, November 2024), meeting that expectation for regulars supports the lifetime value you already have.
